The key driver for this decline is the significant increase in Selling, General & Administrative (SG&A) costs, which grew by 58% in 2010 and 55% in 2012, coinciding with the acquisitions.
   - Brainstorming reasons to lower sales costs: Sales force optimization (right-sizing, training, incentives), process automation, technology adoption (CRM), channel optimization, renegotiating vendor contracts, reducing travel/entertainment expenses.

3. Analysis of Exhibit 3 (Prompt B):
   - Exhibit 3 (Breakdown of typical sales person monthly activity): The client's sales reps spend only 17% of their time selling, while competitors spend 35%. A significant portion of the client's sales reps' time is spent on administrative tasks (30%) and order processing (25%).
   - Recommendation: Increase the proportion of time sales reps spend selling. This can be achieved by offloading administrative and order processing tasks to other roles or automating them.

4. New Sales Model Savings (Prompt C):
   - Investment: 148 new account coordinators * $55,000/coordinator = $8,140,000 (approx $8.25M as per interviewer notes).
   - Time saved for Sales Reps: 16 orders/week * 25 minutes/order = 400 minutes/week.
   - Total time worked by sales reps: 40 hours/week * 60 minutes/hour = 2,400 minutes/week.
   - Percentage of time saved: 400 minutes / 2,400 minutes = 1/6 or ~17%.
   - Total Sales Reps salary: 1,500 sales reps * $120,000/rep = $180,000,000.
   - Benefit (savings from increased efficiency/reduced workforce): (1/6) * $180,000,000 = $30,000,000.
   - Net Saving = Benefit - Investment = $30,000,000 - $8,140,000 = $21,860,000 (approx $21.75M as per interviewer notes).

5. Recommendation for CEO (Prompt D):
   "Mr. CEO, our diagnostic reveals that while So Fresh and So Clean has successfully grown through acquisitions, your net margins have declined from 3.1% to 2.4% primarily due to a significant increase in SG&A costs. This is largely driven by an inefficient sales force, where reps spend only 17% of their time selling, compared to 35% for competitors, due to being burdened with administrative and order processing tasks.

   We recommend implementing a new selling model that introduces entry-level account coordinators to handle these non-selling tasks. This will free up your 1,500 sales representatives to focus on customer engagement and sales. This model is projected to generate annual net savings of approximately $21.8 million, significantly improving your operating income.

   While this is a positive step, it's important to acknowledge potential challenges such as managing the transition for the sales force and ensuring customer continuity during the reorganization. We should also explore further opportunities for process automation and continued optimization of your distribution network to sustain long-term profitability."
evaluation: This case tests a candidate's ability to diagnose a specific problem from vague information. Each prompt tests brainstorming and problem-solving skills. Strong candidates will drive the case, perform the cost-benefit analysis accurately, and identify the core issue of sales force inefficiency. Exceptional candidates will also propose innovative solutions, highlight potential risks (e.g., impact of 17% sales force reduction on customer base), and recognize the relative impact of the proposed savings on overall enterprise profitability (e.g., $21M saving is a 2.1% improvement to 2013 operating income, which is small in the grand scheme).

title: Kid Country
description: Our client, Kid Country, is the purveyor of an increasingly unpopular form of hybrid rap/rock/country music. An early boom in the market for awful music allowed him to amass a substantial war chest and offer products (music related) in a variety of areas. His accountants have recently informed him of consistently declining revenues over the last three years and encouraged him to seek outside expertise.
additional_info: Kid Country's key lines of business are: apparel (tasteless t-shirts), beverages (tasteless beer), CD/MP3 sales, and concert ticket sales.
Overall revenue numbers are down, but trends vary across lines of business.
Profitability trends also vary by line of business.
additional_questions: Kid Country would like us to evaluate a proposal he just received from his concert promoters, which would substantially change the business model of his tours - tell us what you think of it. (The promoters have been able to negotiate a unique deal with the venues which host Kid Country - if he is willing to drop ticket prices dramatically (while increasing the venues’ cut of course), they will allow him to perform back-to-back shows (either the same night or on consecutive nights) and will agree to carry exclusively Kid Country’s own “Real American Red White and Blue Lager” for sale during these shows.)
solution:
1. Initial Framework:
   The candidate should propose a framework to analyze Kid Country's declining revenues and profitability across his various business lines. This should include:
   - Revenue Analysis: Breakdown by product line (apparel, beverages, CD/MP3, concert tickets), pricing, volume, market trends for each.
   - Cost Analysis: COGS, operating expenses, marketing costs for each product line.
   - Profitability Analysis: Gross margin, net margin by product line.
   - External Factors: Competition, changing music industry trends (streaming), consumer preferences.
   - Internal Factors: Brand strength, operational efficiency, marketing effectiveness.

2. Analysis of Exhibits 1 & 2:
   - Concert Tickets: Core business, highest revenue and profit, but declining due to Kid Country's music style. This is a critical area to address.
   - Apparel: Low revenue, low profitability, and hasn't improved. This business line appears to be a 'dud'.
   - CD/MP3 Sales: High revenue but declining with low profitability. This is likely due to the shift to digital streaming services (Spotify, Pandora, iTunes) which squeeze artist margins.
   - Beverages: Low current sales but very good profitability. This is a growth opportunity, and it's important to note that this line of business was added since 2011, indicating it's a newer venture.

3. Recommendations for improving Kid Country's business:
   - Shore up the core concert ticket business: Explore strategies to revitalize interest, potentially through new music, collaborations, or unique concert experiences.
   - Capitalize on beverages: Invest in marketing and distribution for the 'Real American Red White and Blue Lager' given its high profitability and growth potential.
   - Re-evaluate Apparel: Consider discontinuing or significantly restructuring the apparel business due to its poor performance.
   - Address CD/MP3 sales: Acknowledge the industry shift and potentially focus on maximizing revenue from streaming platforms rather than traditional sales, or explore direct-to-fan digital sales.

4. Evaluation of Promoter's Proposal (Prompt #2):
   - The proposal involves dramatically dropping ticket prices, increasing venue's cut, performing back-to-back shows, and exclusively selling Kid Country's beer.
   - To evaluate, compare the current tour profit with the proposed new tour profit.
   - Current Tour Profit (from Exhibit 1 & 2, assuming 2014 is representative of current): 
     - Ticket Revenue: $4M
     - Ticket Margin: $3M
     - Other Costs: $1M
     - Current Concert Profit = $3M - $1M = $2M

   - Proposed New Tour Profit (using Exhibit 3 data):
     - Tour Dates: 100
     - Tickets/Show: 2000
     - Ticket Price: $20
     - Ticket Revenue per show: 2000 * $20 = $40,000
     - Total Ticket Revenue: 100 * $40,000 = $4,000,000
     - Ticket Margin: 50% (from Exhibit 3)
     - Ticket Profit: $4,000,000 * 50% = $2,000,000

     - Beers/Show: 1000
     - Beer Price: $10
     - Beer Revenue per show: 1000 * $10 = $10,000
     - Total Beer Revenue: 100 * $10,000 = $1,000,000
     - Beer Margin: 80% (from Exhibit 3)
     - Beer Profit: $1,000,000 * 80% = $800,000

     - Total Profit from New Tour = Ticket Profit + Beer Profit = $2,000,000 + $800,000 = $2,800,000

   - Comparison: The proposed new tour generates $2.8M profit, which is higher than the current $2M profit. This is a significant increase, especially considering the declining trend of the current business.

   - Go/No-Go Recommendation:
     - Go: The proposal offers a major revenue and profit opportunity, moving Kid Country into a higher profitability space and potentially reversing the disastrous trend of his core business. It leverages the high profitability of the beverage business.
     - Risks/Cons: This fundamentally redefines Kid Country's core business, essentially making him a brewer who uses music to sell beer. There are questions about Kid Country's ability to scale his beverage business and the personal burden of performing twice as many shows. The long-term impact on his brand as a musician needs to be considered.

   - Conclusion: Kid Country should accept the proposal. The financial upside is substantial and provides a much-needed boost to his declining business. While it shifts his brand focus, it capitalizes on a high-margin product and offers a viable path forward. He should be prepared for the increased workload and the strategic shift in his career. Further analysis would involve assessing the scalability of the beverage production and distribution.
evaluation:
Skills tested:
- Rudimentary case math, identification of trends, strengths, and weaknesses within a business, ability to drive a line of thought to an actionable conclusion.
Strong candidates will:
- Complete necessary math with minimal stumbles.
- Handle Exhibit 3 with a clean, well-organized profit calculation table while avoiding unnecessary calculations.
- Consider qualitative factors (e.g., Kid Country's willingness to work more, potential re-branding).
- Drive to conclusions: Recognize that Prompt #2 is a go/no-go decision and present a crisp, concise, and decisive conclusion upon its completion.
- Incorporate data on the relative impact of the project in the conclusion (e.g., circle back to total 2014 profits across all lines of business to show % profit increase in overall terms).

title: Texas Oil
description:
Your client, Texas Oil, is a large, integrated oil and gas company which competes with the likes of ExxonMobil, Shell, and Chevron. They have assets in 72 countries, on 5 continents, and own one-quarter of the US oil market. They recently purchased a large area of land from a small, independent oil company and are attempting to integrate it into their portfolio in West Texas. They hired your firm to help them determine how to develop the purchased land.

While the company is large, it is “capital constrained”. This is because of low crude oil prices and an over-budget refinery in Angola. As a result, the company cannot develop all of the purchased land in the coming years.

How would you frame this problem for the client?
additional_info: All of the purchased land has proven oil and gas reserves (with 90% confidence).
Other major competitors are also in West Texas.
Oil and Gas are commodities, and traded on an international, liquid market (meaning the quality of the reserves is not important for the case).
Texas Oil does not have enough market share to influence the price of oil/gas.
The purchased lease will expire in 3 years.
The total amount purchased is 320,000 acres.
additional_questions: The land on which the drilling rights were purchased is owned by the government and leased to companies on a five-year timeline. If a lease holder does not develop that land in that time, the lease expires and the lease holder can no longer develop it. Texas Oil’s purchased land has only three years remaining on its lease agreement. How many acres can Texas Oil develop before their lease expires? What percentage of their purchased land does this represent?

OK, now that we’ve determined how much of the land can be developed, we need to prioritize. The company has categorized the land into five “focus areas”, each with 64,000 acres (show exhibit 1 to candidate). How would you go about prioritizing focus areas?

Each one of these areas can be developed independently, but not at the same time. The firm has provided you with exhibits 2 and 3 and asked you to determine its optimal course of action. According to the head of the West Texas Business Unit, the company has 300 million available to spend. How should Texas Oil develop its purchased land?
solution:
1. Framing the Problem:
   The problem should be framed as a prioritization challenge given capital constraints and a time-sensitive lease. The core objective is to maximize the value extracted from the purchased land within the given constraints. A good framework would involve:
   - Market Analysis: Understanding the West Texas oil and gas market, competitor activity, and commodity price outlook.
   - Asset Evaluation: Assessing the potential of the purchased land (reserves, geological characteristics, development costs).
   - Strategic Fit: How the development aligns with Texas Oil's overall strategy, given its capital constraints.
   - Financial Analysis: A cost/benefit analysis for development options, focusing on NPV, capital efficiency, and profitability.
   - Operational Feasibility: Development timelines, resource availability, and regulatory considerations.

2. Acres Developed and Percentage (Prompt #2):
   - Historical development rate: 100 square miles per year.
   - Lease remaining: 3 years.
   - Total square miles that can be developed: 100 sq miles/year * 3 years = 300 sq miles.
   - Conversion to acres: 1 square mile = 640 acres.
   - Total acres that can be developed: 300 sq miles * 640 acres/sq mile = 192,000 acres.
   - Total purchased land: 320,000 acres.
   - Percentage of purchased land that can be developed: 192,000 acres / 320,000 acres = 0.60 or 60%.

3. Prioritizing Focus Areas (Prompt #3):
   The primary criterion for prioritization should be maximizing profitability, typically measured by Net Present Value (NPV). However, given the capital constraint, capital efficiency is also crucial. Other considerations include:
   - NPV: Prioritize areas with the highest expected NPV (revenue from reserves minus development costs).
   - Capital Efficiency: Ratio of NPV to Total Cost (NPV/Cost) – this helps identify projects that generate the most value per dollar invested.
   - Total Production: While profit-focused, some companies might prioritize production volume for market share or valuation.
   - Strategic Importance: Are certain areas strategically important for future development or infrastructure?
   - Risk Profile: Geological risk, operational risk, regulatory risk.

4. Optimal Course of Action (Prompt #4):
   - Objective: Maximize profitability (NPV) given a capital budget of $300 million.
   - From Exhibit 2:
     - Eagle Mountain: Cost $94M, NPV $45M
     - Fox Path: Cost $108M, NPV $50M
     - Midland: Cost $96M, NPV $45M
     - Nag River Head: Cost $150M, NPV $55M
     - Coyote Tooth: Cost $110M, NPV $50M

   - From Exhibit 3 (Capital Efficiencies - NPV/Cost):
     - Eagle Mountain: 0.48
     - Fox Path: 0.46
     - Midland: 0.47
     - Nag River Head: 0.37
     - Coyote Tooth: 0.45

   - Strategy: Select areas with high NPV and high capital efficiency, staying within the $300M budget.

   - Option 1: Eagle Mountain + Midland + Fox Path
     - Total Cost: $94M + $96M + $108M = $298M (within budget)
     - Total NPV: $45M + $45M + $50M = $140M

   - Option 2: Eagle Mountain + Midland + Coyote Tooth
     - Total Cost: $94M + $96M + $110M = $300M (within budget)
     - Total NPV: $45M + $45M + $50M = $140M

   - Nag River Head, despite having the highest individual NPV ($55M), is less capital efficient (0.37) and expensive ($150M). Including it would limit the number of other areas that can be developed within the budget. For example, Nag River Head + Eagle Mountain = $150M + $94M = $244M (NPV $55M + $45M = $100M). This leaves $56M, not enough for another full area, resulting in lower total NPV than Option 1 or 2.

   - Optimal Course of Action: Develop Eagle Mountain, Midland, and Fox Path (or Coyote Tooth). Both combinations yield the same total NPV of $140M and stay within the $300M budget. The choice between Fox Path and Coyote Tooth might depend on other qualitative factors not provided.

   - Second-level insights:
     - Texas Oil should consider selling the remaining 40% (128,000 acres) of undeveloped land to other companies before the lease expires to monetize the asset.
     - Re-categorizing land within segments could potentially achieve higher portfolio efficiencies.
     - Explore options to increase the capital budget or extend the lease if highly profitable areas are being left undeveloped.
evaluation:
The case evaluates the candidate's ability to structure a complex problem, perform quantitative analysis, and make strategic recommendations under constraints. An exceptional candidate will:
- Frame the problem effectively, considering both financial and operational aspects.
- Accurately perform the calculations for acres developed.
- Brainstorm a comprehensive list of prioritization criteria, including capital efficiency.
- Correctly identify the optimal combination of focus areas based on NPV and budget constraints.
- Provide second-level insights beyond the direct question, such as selling undeveloped land or exploring budget flexibility.

title: Consumer Products Strategy
description:
Our client is a large, multinational consumer products company with business in over 200 countries. Today, we are going to focus on its US business. Our client has been following US demographic trends and has found that low income households have been growing two times as quickly as other consumer segments. Low income is defined as families with income at the poverty level or below. Our client has always had a premium product strategy. It sells its products in grocery stores, convenience stores, mass retailers, etc. but its products are always priced at the high-end of their respective categories. It has never targeted the low income segment before and doesn't have a low income strategy, but given the growth of this segment, our client is considering entering the low income segment. Our client has 3 questions for BCG:
1) Should it have a low income strategy?
2) If it should have a low income strategy, what are some tactics it should deploy?
3) What are some of the risks the client may face?
additional_info: Low income consumers purchase largely in smaller, local shops.
Low income consumers can't afford salons, but will indulge on shampoos.
Low income consumers are willing to spend more on baby food to protect their children.
There are valid generics that compete with our client's cold medicine.
additional_questions: What would you like to know to help the client answer its questions? (When the candidate asks about products, share Exhibit 1. The client has shared some data for its key products. What do you make of this information?)
What else would you like to know about diapers and cold medicine to assess the opportunity?
What do you see that could help us figure out why low income consumers aren't buying our client's diapers?
What else should our client think about as it develops a low income strategy? What are the risks?
solution:
1. Initial Information Gathering (Prompt 1):
   To answer the client's questions, the candidate should ask about:
   - Market Size & Growth: Specific size and growth rates of the low-income segment for relevant product categories.
   - Client's Product Portfolio: Which products are most relevant or could be adapted for the low-income segment.
   - Competitors: Who currently serves this segment, their pricing, product offerings, and distribution.
   - Customer Needs: Specific needs, purchasing habits, and price sensitivity of low-income consumers.
   - Client Capabilities: Ability to adapt products, distribution, and marketing for this segment without cannibalizing premium brands.

   Analysis of Exhibit 1:
   - Diapers: High market share (30%), high price premium (2x competitors), but low penetration in LI households (10%). This suggests a significant opportunity if the price barrier can be overcome.
   - Cold Medicine: High market share (35%), high price premium (1.5x competitors), but low penetration in LI households (15%). Similar to diapers, but cold medicine often has generic alternatives.
   - Shampoo: Moderate market share (20%), low price premium (1.1x competitors), moderate penetration in LI households (25%). This product is already somewhat accessible to LI consumers.
   - Baby Food: Low market share (15%), low price premium (1.1x competitors), moderate penetration in LI households (20%). This category might be less attractive due to lower market share and premium.
   - Key takeaway: Diapers and Cold Medicine show the largest gap between overall market share and LI penetration, indicating the biggest potential opportunity if the price premium can be addressed.

2. Further Information on Diapers and Cold Medicine (Prompt 2):
   - Diapers:
     - Price elasticity of demand for LI consumers.
     - Current purchasing habits (e.g., buying smaller packs, specific brands, store types).
     - Importance of brand vs. price for LI consumers.
     - Distribution channels preferred by LI consumers.
   - Cold Medicine:
     - Availability and effectiveness of generic alternatives.
     - Importance of brand vs. price for LI consumers in this category.
     - Health literacy and access to healthcare for LI consumers.

   (Interviewer provides: Client is not interested in cold medicine, but LI consumers are interested in buying diapers.)

3. Why LI Consumers Aren't Buying Client's Diapers (Prompt 3):
   - From Exhibit 2:
     - Client's Diapers: Price $20, 40 diapers/pack, $0.50/diaper.
     - Competitor A: Price $15, 30 diapers/pack, $0.50/diaper.
     - Competitor B: Price $10, 20 diapers/pack, $0.50/diaper.
   - Observation: All brands have the same price per diaper ($0.50/diaper). However, the client's product has the highest absolute price per pack ($20) and the largest pack size (40 diapers).
   - Conclusion: Low-income consumers are likely not buying the client's diapers due to the high absolute price point of the package, even though the unit price is competitive. They may lack the upfront capital to purchase a large pack, preferring smaller, more affordable packs.

4. Additional Considerations and Risks (Prompt 4):
   - Tactics for a low-income strategy:
     - Product Adaptation: Offer smaller pack sizes (e.g., 20-count for $10) to reduce the absolute price barrier, while maintaining the unit price.
     - Distribution: Partner with smaller, local shops and discount retailers where LI consumers frequently shop.
     - Marketing: Develop targeted messaging that emphasizes value and accessibility, potentially using different brand names or sub-brands to avoid diluting the premium brand.
     - Cost Structure: Explore ways to reduce production costs for the LI segment to maintain margins on lower-priced products.
   - Risks:
     - Cannibalization: Premium customers might switch to the lower-priced LI product, eroding overall margins.
     - Brand Dilution: Associating the brand with a 'low-income' offering could damage its premium image.
     - Operational Complexity: Managing a dual product strategy (premium and LI) with different distribution and marketing channels can be complex and costly.
     - Competitive Response: Existing LI competitors might react aggressively.
     - Profitability: Ensuring sufficient margins on lower-priced products.

5. Recommendation:
   The client should pursue a low-income strategy, focusing on diapers, which represents a significant untapped market opportunity. The primary tactic should be to reduce the package size to lower the absolute price point (e.g., a 20-diaper pack for $10), making it accessible to low-income consumers while maintaining the competitive unit price. This could generate an additional $25M in revenue (assuming 10% penetration increase in LI households, which is 2x current, and a market size of $1.25B for LI diapers). Key risks include potential cannibalization of existing premium sales and brand dilution, which can be mitigated through careful brand architecture and distinct distribution channels. The client should also explore partnerships with local retailers to reach this segment effectively.
evaluation:
The case evaluates the candidate's ability to structure a market entry problem, analyze data, identify key insights, and propose actionable recommendations while considering risks. An exceptional candidate will:
- Ask insightful clarifying questions to narrow down the problem.
- Quickly identify the core issue from Exhibit 1 (high price premium, low LI penetration) and Exhibit 2 (high absolute price of client's diaper packs).
- Propose a clear, data-driven solution (smaller pack size for diapers).
- Articulate a comprehensive list of tactics and risks for a low-income strategy.
- Provide a concise and well-supported recommendation.

title: Pharmaceutical Rare Disease Business Growth
description: Our client is a large pharmaceutical company with a strong business in "Rare Disease" (rare diseases are conditions that affect fewer than 200,000 people in the US). Historically, pharmaceutical companies have not invested significantly in Rare Disease because it has not been cost effective to conduct research for such small populations. Due to the fall off in the number of pharmaceutical blockbusters and improved regulation to make R&D cheaper for Rare Disease, pharmaceutical companies have begun to invest more in Rare Disease R&D over the last few years. Our client has hired BCG because it would like to grow its Rare Disease business. How can the client double its Rare Disease business in 5 years?
additional_info: Our client is an industry leader with a track record of success. Its existing business is $2.5B, expected to grow at 5% per year.
Rare disease market is $30B and is as profitable as "Big Pharma".
While research is relatively expensive, the government provides tax credits for drugs designated to treat rare diseases.
Additionally, prices tend to be higher to make up for the upfront research investment. It's also growing faster than Big Pharma.
There are many small bio-techs that are attractive targets and there are also other big pharmaceutical companies that have rare disease divisions / assets.
additional_questions: How would you approach our client's question on growing its Rare Disease business?
Where would you like to start?

When the candidate asks about the existing business, share Exhibit 1. Based on the data, how large will the client's rare disease business be in 2016? [If necessary,] what is the shortfall to its goal of doubling its business?
What does the client need to do to double the Rare Disease revenues by 2016? [If candidates discusses acquisitions] ask who should the client acquire and how many assets will client need to buy?
Let's say the CEO walks in, could you summarize for me?
solution:
The client's current rare disease business is $2.5B. The goal is to double it in 5 years, meaning a target of $5B by 2016.

1. **Organic Growth Calculation:**
   Current business: $2.5B
   Annual growth rate: 5%
   Growth over 5 years: $2.5B * (1 + 0.05)^5 = $2.5B * 1.276 = $3.19B
   
   So, by 2016, the client's rare disease business will be approximately $3.19B through organic growth.

2. **Shortfall to Goal:**
   Target revenue: $5B
   Projected organic revenue: $3.19B
   Shortfall: $5B - $3.19B = $1.81B

3. **Addressing the Shortfall (Acquisitions):**
   To close the $1.81B gap, the client will need to pursue inorganic growth, likely through acquisitions. The case implies that there are many small biotech targets and other big pharma companies with rare disease assets.
   
   Assuming an average 'peak revenue' per acquired asset (this would need to be estimated or provided, but the solution states ~3 assets), the client would need to acquire assets totaling approximately $1.81B in peak revenue.
   
   If we assume an average peak revenue of $600M per asset (e.g., $1.81B / 3 assets), then approximately 3 assets would be needed.

**Recommendation:**
Internal growth will not be sufficient to achieve your goal of doubling your business. You will need to acquire approximately 3 "peak revenue" assets to close the gap between your goal of $5B and the $3.19B your portfolio will generate organically. The client should focus on identifying attractive small biotech targets or rare disease divisions/assets from other pharmaceutical companies that can contribute the necessary revenue to reach the $5B target by 2016.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate a structured approach to problem-solving, including breaking down the problem into organic and inorganic growth. They should be able to perform accurate calculations for future revenue and the shortfall. For the acquisition part, they should logically deduce the need for acquisitions and potentially estimate the number of assets needed based on reasonable assumptions (or ask for more data). The final summary should be concise and actionable.

title: InsureCo – Business Restructuring
description:
Our client, InsureCo, is a large, multi-national insurance provider, with revenues of over $25B in 2009. However, the tightening of the credit markets and downturn in the economy has caused their profitability to drop significantly from 10% to 5% in the most recent year. As a result of economic downturn, our client has decided to evaluate their business portfolio and divest portions of their business to raise capital and refocus on their core General Insurance business. Their goal is to raise over $3B in capital through the sale of various business units. The management team has already identified two potential candidates for divestiture from its Life Insurance and Property and Casualty divisions. It is considering divesting one of the following business units, in addition to several other businesses across the company:

LifeLine Asia: Asian-based life insurance business, with $250MM in annual revenues
All-World Property and Casualty: A US-based Property and Casualty insurance business, with $100MM in annual revenues

Deloitte Consulting has been engaged to identify and recommend the most viable business unit candidates for divestiture, as well as assist in the operational separation of the business units’ operations from InsureCo.

1. What would you estimate as the potential sales price for each company? Please use available data to support your argument (Note to interviewer: no other data is provided, candidate can make assumptions as necessary)
2. Which company would you advise InsureCo to divest?
3. What are some of the other key factors that InsureCo should consider when assessing which units to divest?
additional_info: Data Sheet (Exhibit on page 253)
additional_questions: What would you estimate as the potential sales price for each company? Please use available data to support your argument (Note to interviewer: Please provide candidate with data sheet)
Which company would you advise InsureCo to divest?
What are some of the other key factors that InsureCo should consider when assessing which units to divest?
solution:
1. **Potential Sales Price Estimation:**
   The candidate should use the provided data sheet (Exhibit on page 253) which contains P/E multiples for Life and P&C insurance sectors, and potentially regional trends.

   *Initial Approach (using average P/E multiples):*
   - **LifeLine Asia:**
     - Annual Revenues: $250MM
     - Assuming a typical profit margin (e.g., 10% as per InsureCo's historical profitability, or a more conservative industry average if assumed by candidate), let's estimate Net Income.
     - If Net Income is 10% of revenue: $250MM * 0.10 = $25MM
     - Average P/E for Life Insurance (from data sheet, assume 12x for simplicity if no specific year is given initially, or use 2009 P/E of 12x): $25MM * 12 = $300MM
   - **All-World Property and Casualty:**
     - Annual Revenues: $100MM
     - Assuming a typical profit margin (e.g., 10%): $100MM * 0.10 = $10MM
     - Average P/E for P&C Insurance (from data sheet, assume 15x for simplicity or use 2009 P/E of 15x): $10MM * 15 = $150MM

   *Better Answer (considering P/E trends and regional/segment trends):*
   - The P/E for Life has been declining (from 15x in 2007 to 10x in 2009), while P&C has been increasing (from 10x in 2007 to 20x in 2009).
   - Using more recent (2009) multiples:
     - **LifeLine Asia:** Net Income (assuming 10% margin) = $25MM. P/E (2009 Life) = 10x. Potential Price = $25MM * 10 = $250MM.
     - **All-World P&C:** Net Income (assuming 10% margin) = $10MM. P/E (2009 P&C) = 20x. Potential Price = $10MM * 20 = $200MM.
   - This makes LifeLine Asia potentially more valuable based on revenue, but All-World P&C has a higher P/E multiple.

   *Great Answer (incorporating industry and geography trends from data sheet):*
   - **LifeLine Asia:** Life segment is declining, but Asia geography is growing. This could lead to a higher premium for an Asian asset despite the declining segment, or a discount due to the segment. The P/E trend for Life is negative.
   - **All-World P&C:** P&C segment is growing, but North America geography is declining. The P/E trend for P&C is positive. This could lead to a higher valuation due to segment growth, but potentially offset by regional decline.
   - The candidate should articulate these conflicting trends and make a reasoned assumption for valuation, possibly adjusting the P/E multiples based on these factors.

2. **Which company to advise InsureCo to divest?**
   The choice depends on the candidate's interpretation of the data and strategic priorities. Both options have pros and cons.

   *If choosing LifeLine Asia:*
   - **Higher potential sales price:** Based on initial revenue and average P/E, it might fetch a higher absolute price ($250MM vs $200MM using 2009 P/E and 10% margin). This helps meet the $3B capital goal.
   - **Strategic exit from declining segment:** Life insurance is a declining segment, so divesting allows InsureCo to exit a non-core, underperforming area.
   - **Attractive market for buyers:** Asia is a growing market, which might attract more buyers and potentially a higher premium.

   *If choosing All-World Property and Casualty:*
   - **Higher P/E multiple:** P&C has a higher and growing P/E multiple, indicating market optimism for this segment.
   - **Refocus on core:** While P&C is core, divesting a smaller, potentially less strategic US-based P&C unit could streamline operations and allow focus on larger, more profitable P&C segments.
   - **Lower absolute capital raised:** This unit is smaller in revenue, so it will contribute less to the $3B capital goal.

   *Recommendation should consider:* The client's goal is to raise over $3B and refocus on core General Insurance. Divesting LifeLine Asia, despite being in a growing region, aligns with exiting a declining segment (Life Insurance) and potentially raises more capital in absolute terms. Divesting All-World P&C, while in a growing segment (P&C), is in a declining region (North America) and raises less capital. A strong argument could be made for LifeLine Asia to shed a non-core, declining segment and raise more capital, even if the regional growth is attractive to a buyer.

3. **Other key factors for InsureCo to consider:**
   - **Overall Culture/People Impact:** How will the divestiture affect employee morale, retention of key talent, and the company's overall image? Are there employees being groomed for executive positions in the divested unit?
   - **Geographic Reach/Overlap:** Does the divested unit have unique geographic coverage or product offerings that complement other parts of InsureCo's business? Will divesting create gaps?
   - **Impact to Remaining Revenue Streams:** Are there cross-selling opportunities or interdependencies between the divested unit and remaining businesses? What is the potential loss of revenue from these synergies?
   - **Potential Buyers:** Who are the likely buyers? What are their motivations? Are there regulatory or legal hurdles (e.g., anti-trust, spin-off via IPO)?
   - **Shared Infrastructure:** This is a critical point. Many business units rely on shared IT, finance, HR, sales, and marketing systems. What is the cost and complexity of separating these systems? How long will it take? What is the risk of disruption to remaining operations?
   - **Strategic Fit:** How well does the business unit align with InsureCo's long-term strategic vision for its core General Insurance business?
   - **Market Conditions:** Current and projected market conditions for the specific business unit and its geographic region, beyond just P/E multiples.
   - **Tax Implications:** What are the tax consequences of the divestiture?
   - **Transaction Costs:** Legal, advisory, and other costs associated with the sale.

**Conclusion:** The candidate should synthesize these points into a comprehensive recommendation, weighing the financial aspects with strategic and operational considerations.
evaluation: The candidate should demonstrate strong quantitative skills in calculating potential sales prices, making reasonable assumptions where data is missing (e.g., profit margin). They should be able to interpret trends from the provided data sheet (P/E trends, segment/geographic growth). For the recommendation, they should present a balanced view, articulating the pros and cons of each divestiture option and making a clear, well-supported choice. For the 'other factors' question, they should provide a structured and comprehensive list of considerations, ideally touching upon financial, strategic, operational, and human capital aspects. A 'great answer' will specifically highlight the impact of shared infrastructure.

title: YachtCo
description: Your client is YachtCo, a US-based luxury motor yacht manufacturer. Started in 1958, they have grown into one of the largest and most prestigious yacht brands in the country. However, they have seen a gradual decline in their profitability in recent years. Their CEO has come to you to develop a turnaround strategy for the company, aiming to improve profitability for the medium-to-long term. What should they do?
additional_info: YachtCo has 2 core lines of business: (1) design and sale of new boats, (2) maintenance and/or renovation of existing boats.
Their main buyers are high net worth individuals and companies, both domestic and abroad.
Competitors: Stable revenues with slight decline in profits, but not to the same degree as YachtCo.
Customers: market research shows that YachtCo is still maintaining its market share in the luxury yacht segment, despite competition from abroad.
If asked about finances, say that you will return to this later and ask them to complete the framework.
additional_questions:
Prompt #2: Provide the interviewee with the following information:
• New Yacht Sales
– 2014: 100 sales, average sale price $3.2M, average cost $2.6M
– 2015: 110 sales, average sale price $3.3M, average cost $3.0M
• Yacht Repair/ Renovations
– 2014: 200 contracts, average contract $850K, average cost $710K
– 2015: 300 contracts, average contract $900K, average cost $750K

Ask candidate for possible reasons as to why profitability has declined in new yacht sales.

Prompt #3: Given the increased demand for steel in Asia and the Middle East, the price of high-grade steel has jumped over 50% in the past year from $1100 to $1800 per metric ton. Give candidate Exhibit 1. YachtCo’s engineering department recommended that YachtCo transition from traditional steel hulls to newer fiberglass hulls to reduce the costs of construction. Is it worth doing this over the next 5 years?
Prompt #4: Given your recommendation of _________, what other factors should you YachtCo consider when changing hull types? Feel free to take a minute to organize your thoughts before presenting.
Recommendation: The CEO has asked you to summarize your findings and present them to the board. Please make your recommendation.
solution:
Prompt #2: A good candidate will note that profits from new yacht sales have declined, largely due to the increased average costs. They will also note that yacht repairs/ renovations have seen profit growth, however not enough to offset the decline from new yacht sales. Possible reasons for declining profitability in new yacht sales: increased costs (design, raw materials, transport, labor), competitor price pressures restricting sale price increases.

Prompt #3: Calculations for Option 1 (Continue with current steel hull design):
– 30 tons/boat * $1800/ton = $54,000/boat
– $54,000 * 100 boats * 5 years = $27M
Calculations for Option 2 (Transition to fiberglass hulls):
– $40,000 boat * 100 boats * 5 years = $20M
– $20M + $4M up-front cost = $24M
Based on these calculations, it is ideal to go with option 2.

Prompt #4: The key here is to approach this in a systematic method. One such method is:
1) Customer: customer preferences and willingness to pay for different hull type (i.e. is any one type superior?), versatility in design and construction, down-time for yacht construction and potential complications associated with this.
2) Company: cost of transition, repair and maintenance of boats (will this new hull type reduce the need for maintenance, thereby reducing other profit streams), ongoing training requirements, fluctuations in steel costs over time, depreciation opportunities for equipment, brand reputation, etc.
3) Competitors: What is the market standard? Is there a shift towards fiberglass, or possibly even a new material?
A strong candidate will identify that the rising cost of steel does not account for the entire increase in YachtCo’s costs. If the candidate addresses this, inform them that their firm is addressing other costs in a different workstream.

Recommendation: Candidate should summarize several key findings here:
– Identify the unit (new construction) that is adversely affecting company profitability.
– Recognize that the rise in steel prices is driving construction costs up.
– Note that swapping to fiberglass hulls will be more profitable over five years.
Outstanding candidates may also explore:
– Outsource hull construction to 3rd party to keep costs down.
– Look into ways to reduce cost of steel: bulk buying, recycle hulls from old boats, etc.
Candidate should recognize that switching to fiberglass hulls will not provide adequate savings to offset drop in revenue. This is one of the key learning points of the case: the recommendation needs to achieve the CEO objective from Prompt 1.
evaluation: A good candidate will identify the decline in new yacht sales profitability and link it to increased costs. For the quantitative section, they should accurately calculate the costs for both steel and fiberglass options over 5 years and recommend the more cost-effective option. For the qualitative section, they should provide a structured approach to considering additional factors (customer, company, competitors). The final recommendation should be clear, concise, and directly address the CEO's objective of improving profitability.

title: Dam It
description: California is dealing with very harsh drought conditions. It is economically impacting the lives and businesses of its residents. The state financial committee has been presented with a project to capture more water by increasing the size of a current dam, especially given the constraints on their budget and a set of competing state projects in other domains. Currently, California is buying water from Nevada. Though the new project seems promising, the committee wants to ensure the investment is sound. Can you help the state of California evaluate this proposal?
additional_info: The water purchased from Nevada is used across the state to fulfill all needs including wineries and residential use.
There is only one other project for which the state is considering funding with this allocated money.
The second project is an additional wing to the state hospital to be built from the ground up.
The second project is just as socially important as the dam.
additional_questions: Prompt #2: If candidate does not drive to NPV: The state would like to find out the current value to compare projects equally.
Prompt #3: What non-financial factors should we consider when deciding between these two projects?
Recommendation: Your main client, the Governor of California is walking into the room. What is your recommendation?
solution:
Prompt #1: Framework should include (a MECE framework will have Financial, Social & political/other alternatives):
– Candidate should draw on Finance knowledge to compare projects (NPV, IRR, Comps, Multiples).
– Should include Costs and Revenues as this case is centered around profitability for the state at the core.
– Other buckets can include macroeconomic issues, regulatory issues, voting/citizen rights issues etc.

Prompt #2: This is a question of a growing perpetuity. The candidate should realize that they need the investment’s cost and revenue information.
– Initial Investment = 15M
– Yearly Revenues from things like power generation = 1.5M/yr
– Yearly Cost of supplies and Upkeep = 250K
– Yearly SG&A = 750k
– All taxes can be ignored
– Cost of purchasing water from Nevada = 100K
– Candidate should request discount rate and growth rate: Discount Rate = 5%, Growth Rate = 3%
– Candidate should use perpetuity formula (FCF / (R-G))
– Total Revenue = 1.5M + 100K
– FCF = Revenue – Cost of Supplies + SG&A = 1.6M – (250K + 750K) = 600K
– NPV = FCF / (R-G) – Initial Investment = 600K / (5% – 3%) – 15M = 15M

Prompt #3: This is a structured brainstorming exercise. Examples can include: ability to generate more revenue channels from another project like hospital (research etc), building a project from ground up compared to adding on to a project (the dam) for only a 100K difference may not make sense, considering the most critical social and political issues is equally important.

Recommendation: There is no right answer for this case and either project can be selected. Both projects are viable and should be supported by sound reasoning found through brainstorming of the non-financial aspects and calculating the financial impact independently.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate a structured approach to evaluating public sector projects, considering financial (NPV calculation) and non-financial factors. For the NPV calculation, accuracy in applying the perpetuity formula and identifying relevant costs/revenues is key. For non-financial factors, the candidate should provide a comprehensive and well-reasoned list. The final recommendation should be well-supported by the analysis, even if there's no single 'right' answer.

title: Sam’s Sushi
description: You are out to dinner with a colleague at Sam’s Sushi, a Michelin-starred sushi restaurant in Wichita, Kansas. Sam’s rose to prominence due to its unerring commitment to both quality and simplicity (Sam prides himself on only serving two items: sushi and sake). After a few sushi rolls and more glasses of sake than you care to count, your colleague asks you how much you think the restaurant is worth and whether or not buying it would be a good investment. How would you go about determining the value of the restaurant?
additional_info: Only one restaurant
additional_questions:
Prompt #2: 1. How would you estimate Sam’s average revenues? (have candidate lay out potential approaches)
2. What factors might cause variation in revenues? (brainstorm)
3. Present Exhibit 1 and have the candidate calculate the annual revenue

Prompt #3: Now let’s think about Sam’s cash flows. We have the following information about their cost structure and growth rates:
– Staff: A hostess, a waiter, and a sushi chef. All 3 work the entire evening.
– Tax rate: 40%
– Discount rate: 13%
– Annual growth rate of cash flows: 3%
– Variable costs: 20% of total revenue
– Fixed costs: $120,000/year
What are the annual cash flows? What is the value of the restaurant?
Recommendation: So, your colleague needs an answer. What do you tell them about the profitability and valuation of the restaurant? Is it a good investment?
solution:
Prompt #1: Candidate should use a revenue – cost framework to determine cash flows. The value of the restaurant is the present value of the future cash flows. Once candidate has demonstrated that they want to use a revenue – cost framework, prompt them to provide detail about what the revenue and cost buckets consist of:
– For revenues, bucket consists of the number of items (sushi + sake) ordered and their respective prices.
– For costs, there are two components: fixed and variable.
• Possible fixed costs: rent, maintenance, insurance, labor, license fees, etc.
• Variable costs: COGS (rice, fish, soy sauce, wasabi, sake).

Prompt #2: Ways to estimate average revenues:
– By product (e.g., # of rolls sold * avg. price per roll)
– By customer (e.g., # of customers per day * avg. spend per customer)
– By timeframe (e.g., avg. sales per day * total number of operating days per year)
Exhibit 1 Calculation:
Fridays and Saturdays (Spring and Summer): $4,800 * 2 days = $9,600. 25 weeks * $9,600 = $240,000.
Rest of Week (Spring and Summer): $4,800 * (1/6) * 4 days = $3,200. 25 weeks * $3,200 = $80,000.
Fridays and Saturdays (Fall and Winter): (3/4) * $240,000 = $180,000.
Rest of Week (Fall and Winter): 0.85 * $80,000 = $68,000.
Total Annual Revenue = $240,000 + $80,000 + $180,000 + $68,000 = $568,000.

Prompt #3:
– Revenues = $568,000
– Costs = $120,000 (fixed) + (0.2 * $568,000) (variable) = $120,000 + $113,600 = $233,600
– Profit before tax = $568,000 - $233,600 = $334,400
– After-tax cash flow = $334,400 * (1 - 0.40) = $200,640 (rounded to $200,400 in casebook)
– Valuation (perpetuity formula) = Cash Flows / (Discount Rate – Growth Rate) = $200,400 / (0.13 - 0.03) = $200,400 / 0.10 = ~$2M.

Recommendation: Candidate should state the restaurant has ~$200K annual cash flow and is worth ~$2M based on the previous valuation exercise. $2M is a lot for a single restaurant in a small market, and appears to have healthy annual cash flow and low overhead, thus making it a solid investment. Extra points if candidate also considers strategic angles (restaurant must have significant competitive advantage to be doing so well in such a small market with limited access to fish, is most likely sole high-end sushi restaurant in the greater Wichita area, etc.) to further bolster investment rationale.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate a clear understanding of valuation using a revenue-cost framework. They should accurately calculate annual revenue from the exhibit and then calculate annual cash flows and the final valuation using the perpetuity formula. The recommendation should be concise, supported by the quantitative analysis, and ideally include some qualitative strategic considerations.

title: Swipe Right for Canoodle
description: Your client is Canoodle, a revolutionary mobile dating service which utilizes swipe technology allowing users to easily select mates based on their online profiles. Canoodle was started 2 years ago and now operates across the United States. Despite its success and mass popularity, Canoodle has remained unprofitable, and investors are starting to put pressure on the CEO, Stevie McDreamy, to turn the business around. McDreamy has come to you to help out Canoodle. What should they do?
additional_info: Canoodle only operates within the United States with ambitions to expand internationally.
The app is free to download off all mobile application stores. Canoodle’s main source of revenue so far has been through ad sales.
The company must show a profit within 2 years.
Canoodle is already an LGBT friendly dating app and caters to the sexual orientation of all its users.

The market for dating applications consists of ~4 major national competitors. Most users typically have 1-3 dating apps, and all 4 competitors have roughly the same number of users within the United States.
Canoodle does not consider traditional online dating websites in the same market.
additional_questions: Prompt #2: Upon hearing your suggestions, McDreamy is inclined to pursue a “premium” service for consumers to use. The development teams estimates costs at $500,000 and would take 6 months to develop. What factors should McDreamy consider before committing to the investment?

Prompt #3: Due to staff and server capacity issues, McDreamy has decided that he would like to launch in just one city this year before rolling out nationwide. Which city would be the best for CanoodlePremium to launch in?
Recommendation: What is your recommendation for Canoodle?
solution:
Prompt #1: Interviewee should pick up on the fact that revenues have fallen with the slower growth in the user base. Additionally, costs have stabilized. This is a Revenue problem. Ask Candidate to brainstorm sources of revenue:
– Selling user demographic data (risk of potential privacy issues)
– Selling outside services (i.e. emojis/stickers)
– Selling a premium upgrade to its users
– Charging on the application store

Prompt #2: Candidate should pick up on difficulties entering new market:
– Price
– Market size
– Adoption rate
– Marketing
Bonus points if they pick up on the following:
– Customer churn/impacts on current customer base
– Transaction costs
– Implementation timeline and competitive response

Prompt #3: Provide candidate with exhibit. If the candidate asks, the price for the premium service per month is $3. McDreamy wants the initiative to breakeven within 6 months of launching.
Calculations for 6-month revenue (User base * Adoption rate * $3/month * 6 months):
– New York: 300,000 * 0.09 * $3 * 6 = $486,000
– Los Angeles: 350,000 * 0.08 * $3 * 6 = $504,000
– Chicago: 200,000 * 0.10 * $3 * 6 = $360,000
– Houston: 175,000 * 0.09 * $3 * 6 = $283,500
– Washington D.C.: 230,000 * 0.07 * $3 * 6 = $289,800
LA is the only city that breaks even (revenue >= $500,000 development cost) in 6 months.

Recommendation: Canoodle should launch a premium service in Los Angeles.
Concerns:
– Sensitivity around adoption rate
– Risk of launching in one city versus all at once
– Extra maintenance costs for premium service
– Competitive response
Next Steps:
– Development (in house or contracted)
evaluation: Candidate should identify the core problem as revenue-related and brainstorm diverse revenue streams. For the qualitative factors, they should provide a structured and comprehensive list of considerations for launching a new service. Quantitatively, they must accurately calculate the 6-month revenue for each city and identify the city that meets the breakeven target. The final recommendation should be clear, supported by the analysis, and include potential risks and next steps.

title: Cackalacky Construction
description: Cackalacky Construction is a small general contractor in the southeast US. They build schools exclusively. In 2016 Cackalacky generated $20M in revenue, but they have seen stagnant growth in recent years. Cackalacky Construction is looking to double their revenue in the next 3 years and has hired you to advise them how to do it.
additional_info: General contractors are responsible for all building activities but not design or engineering. They supervise subcontractors who perform physical construction work.
Cackalacky clients are local governments that receive federal and state funding to build schools; Cackalacky only builds public schools and there aren’t any private clients.
Cackalacky Construction has been in the southeast US for 20 years.
Contractors who build schools in this region are facing similar problems.
additional_questions: Prompt #2: Cackalacky Construction has decided to enter the Texas market. What are some of the considerations to enter? What is the best way to enter the market?
solution:
Prompt #1: Next step: The discussion on the initial framework should lead to analyzing potential markets to enter. Handover Exhibit # 1 at that point.
Exhibit #1 Guidance:
– Funding amount is the total market size for schools in respective state, no other funding.
– Funding growth rate is the same every year for 3 years.
– Anticipated market penetration for Cackalacky is based on historical GC entrance into market.
– Rounding is acceptable for funding $.
– Important for Interviewee to remember goal of doubling revenue in three years (additional $20M).
– Interviewee should do math to get state funding (I e: market size) for three years.
– Potential time consuming approach: Interviewee multiplies penetration rates for every year instead of just 2019.
– Interviewee should multiply 2019 state funding by appropriate penetration rates.
– Texas should offer the biggest market opportunity ($26M).
– Georgia should also meet goal ($20M) of doubling revenue by 2019.
– Interviewee should realize Texas and Georgia meet goal.
– Rationalize best opportunity and provide recommendation.

Calculations for 2019 Projected Additional Revenue:
Michigan: $100M * (1.10)^3 * 0.10 = $133.1M * 0.10 = $13.31M
Texas: $150M * (1.20)^3 * 0.10 = $259.2M * 0.10 = $25.92M
Georgia: $30M * (1.50)^3 * 0.20 = $101.25M * 0.20 = $20.25M

Prompt #2: Interviewee needs a new framework; some form of Build, Borrow, Buy. The consideration to enter will include understanding the market forces, including the competition. When the discussion is around Competition, handover Exhibit # 2 and analyze the competitive landscape in Texas.
evaluation: Candidate should develop a structured approach to growth strategy, likely involving market analysis. For the quantitative section, they must accurately calculate the projected additional revenue for each state based on the provided exhibit and identify which states meet the revenue doubling goal. For the qualitative section, they should propose a framework for market entry considerations (e.g., Build, Borrow, Buy) and discuss competitive landscape analysis.

title: Polar Bear Pool Float
description: Your client is Hot Koala, a leading producer of outdoor lifestyle products (e.g., games, towels, chairs, umbrellas) based in Dubai. It is looking to make a “splash” (pun intended) with its newest product line– the Polar Bear Pool Float (PBPF). Its current and loyal customer base is entirely in the Middle East, but it is strongly considering new market opportunities. The founder / CFO (Chief Fun Officer) is torn on where to launch the PBPF and has asked us how we would evaluate new geographies.
additional_info: Competitors: Hot Koala has several smaller, niche competitors, but none offer the breadth of products.
Global Market: Hot Koala's current customer base is entirely in the Middle East.
Annual Revenue: $10M per year
Existing Distribution Channels: exclusively ecommerce
additional_questions: Based on Exhibit #1, which countries should Hot Koala prioritize (i.e., top 2) to explore further and why? What other factors should be considered?

Now that we have focused on the US and UAE, let’s explore production and shipping costs of each geography. Hot Koala works with a manufacturer in China for its other product lines and would like to continue partnering with this manufacturer for the PBPF. The manufacturer handles production and can handle shipping to the UAE, but previous research has shown that it would be price prohibitive to use the manufacturer to ship directly to customers in the US. As a result, bundles of PBPFs would have to be shipped to a US entity that would handle storage and fulfillment of orders for a fee. What are some potential cost drivers?
We currently have initial inventory of 50k floats. If we were to sell all of these at a 10% premium over costs, what would our initial market share be in each target market?
Make a recommendation as to where Hot Koala should attempt to commercialize its PBPF.
solution: Prompt #1: The candidate should develop a market entry framework considering factors like market size, growth potential, competition, distribution channels, costs (production, shipping, taxes), time to market, weather, and access to pools/beaches. Prompt #2: Based on Exhibit 1, the US and UAE are strong candidates. The US has a significant market size (10M floats sold), while the UAE has strong market growth (20% YoY) and premium pricing ($99 avg. price). Other factors to consider include production and shipping, taxes, intellectual property restrictions, and distribution channels. Prompt #3: Cost drivers include: materials ($15/float), labor ($10/float), packaging ($5/float), shipping (US: $8000/1000 floats = $8/float; UAE: $5.25/float), storage (US: $2000/500 floats = $4/float; UAE: $1500/750 floats = $2/float), taxes (US: $6/float; UAE: $2.75/float), and distribution fees (US: 50% of storage fee = $2/float; UAE: N/A). Unit costs: US = $15+$10+$5+$8+$4+$6+$2 = $50/float. UAE = $15+$10+$5+$5.25+$2+$2.75 = $40/float. Prompt #4: US Revenue: 50,000 floats * $50/float * 1.1 (10% premium) = $2,750,000. US Market Share: $2.75M / ($50 avg. price * 10M total floats) = $2.75M / $500M = 0.55%. UAE Revenue: 50,000 floats * $40/float * 1.1 (10% premium) = $2,200,000. UAE Market Share: $2.2M / ($99 avg. price * 0.25M total floats) = $2.2M / $24.75M = 8.89%. Recommendation: Hot Koala should prioritize the UAE. While the US market is larger, the cost structure makes it difficult to achieve a significant margin at the current average selling price. The UAE offers higher average selling prices, strong growth, and Hot Koala has existing brand recognition and experience in the Middle East, allowing for a higher market share and better profitability. For the US, an alternate strategy or product differentiation would be needed to justify entry.
evaluation: Candidate should present a structured market entry framework. For Exhibit 1, they should identify key metrics (market size, growth, price) and justify their top 2 choices. For cost drivers, they should brainstorm comprehensively and accurately calculate unit costs. For market share, calculations must be correct. The final recommendation should be clear, well-supported by data, and address potential risks and next steps.

title: Sardine Airlines
description: Sardine Airlines is an ultra low-cost carrier with flights throughout the continental United States. They have hub airports in Oakland, California; Tulsa, Oklahoma, and Hartford, Connecticut. Sardine Airlines is facing increased pressure from other low cost carriers such as Cattle Car Air and Soul Airlines. Sardine Airlines has faced declining profit for the past year. Sardine’s CEO, Penny McPincher, has asked your team for advice on how to reverse the profitability trend.
additional_info: Sardine Airlines competes primarily on having the lowest cost fares and offering minimal service.
Due to its business model Sardine Airlines has a culture of cost savings that can be passed to the customer.
Sardine Airlines is trying to grow profit margin to 20% (net income/total revenue).
additional_questions: Sardine Airlines has been aggressively advertising to combat competitive pressures. Both the CEO and the Board believe this is a critical expenditure. Recently the landlord for the firm’s headquarters in Oakland raised rent by $35M. Customer service complaints have increased nearly 3,000%, which the company believes is due to the new 12 inch seats that were installed in the entire fleet. This has caused Sardine’s call center provider to increase billing by 520% from 2014’s $12.36M. What can Sardine Airlines do to address these issues?
The CEO, Ms. McPincher is going to be joining us in just a few minutes to hear your recommendations on how to improve profitability.
solution: Prompt #1: The candidate should analyze the provided Statement of Operations (Exhibit 1) to identify the drivers of declining profit. Key observations should include: total revenue is growing, but net income is declining. SG&A as a percentage of total revenue increased significantly from 15% in 2014 to 20% in 2015, making it the primary driver of declining profit. Maintenance costs also increased, but Exhibit 3 clarifies this was due to FAA intervention and is not a target for cuts. Prompt #2: To address the issues: 1. Rent: The firm does not have to be headquartered in Oakland. Moving headquarters to Tulsa or Hartford could save 40% of the current 2015 rent, which is $34.4M (40% of $86M). 2. Customer Service: The call center provider charges 60% higher rates due to being US-based with native English speakers. The 2015 customer service cost is $64M (from Exhibit 2). If the 2014 cost was $12.36M, a 520% increase means 2015 cost is $12.36M * (1 + 5.2) = $76.63M. The prompt states 2015 cost is $64M. Assuming the 520% increase is from a base of $12.36M, the current cost is $64M. Moving to an overseas vendor could save a significant portion of this, potentially around $38M (e.g., if costs are reduced by 60%). The company is not interested in increasing seat sizes or cutting marketing expenses. Recommendation: To achieve the 20% profit margin target, Sardine Airlines should focus on cutting SG&A costs. Specifically, recommend moving the headquarters to a less expensive location like Tulsa or Hartford to save approximately $34.4M in rent. Additionally, recommend transitioning to an overseas-based call center vendor to save approximately $38M in customer service costs. These combined savings of over $70M would significantly contribute to reaching the profit target. Risks include one-time moving expenses, potential customer dissatisfaction from outsourced customer service, and the need for careful communication with employees and customers. The company should also reconsider its strategy of installing 8-inch seats given the negative impact of 12-inch seats on customer complaints.
evaluation: Candidate should quickly identify SG&A as the primary driver of declining profitability from Exhibit 1. For Prompt 2, they should accurately calculate potential savings from rent and customer service, demonstrating strong quantitative skills. The final recommendation should be clear, actionable, and directly address the client's objective, while also considering risks and providing a structured approach.

title: The Run of the Mill NOWOCO
description: Our client is a Germany-based, material manufacturer called ‘The Run of the Mill NOWOCO’ specializing in a range of products for the construction industry. In recent months, our client has benefited from anti-dumping taxes on the import of similar products. This has resulted in a demand surge for our client and other domestic competitors; however overall profitability is going down and customer complaints about order fulfilment are at an all-time high. As an integral part of your team, what do you think is going on here?
additional_info: The ultimate goals are to maximize overall profitability & order fulfilment % for key customers.
Key customers will be the ones contributing the most to profitability.
The client sells products (specific product lines are irrelevant for this case) for the construction industry. It sells directly to customers & through distributors in Germany only.
The client is one of the 3 largest domestic producers with almost 30% market share.
The client defines: Profitability over variable costs, that is, profitability = R – VC; Order fulfilment % is defined as: (% of tons delivered on time / total tons ordered).
additional_questions: What are some ways the client can increase the order fulfillment as defined previously? What are some of the trade-offs of doing that?
How can the client prioritize customers and improve the order fulfillment? What information will be required to make this decision?
Based on Exhibit 2, calculate the best case profitability and order fulfilment % for the customers assuming no demand constraints
What are the risks of suggesting this strategy to focus on the top 3 customers only? What are some of the ways these risks could be mitigated?
You walked into an elevator with the CEO. What would your final recommendation be?
solution: Prompt #1: The candidate should develop a framework to diagnose the profitability and order fulfillment issues. This should cover: Financial aspects (revenue, variable costs, pricing strategy, impact of anti-dumping taxes), Operational issues (manufacturing capacity, utilization, bottlenecks, logistics, supply chain, inventory management, quality control), and External factors (competitor actions, market demand, customer segments). The demand surge likely strained existing capacity, leading to higher costs (e.g., overtime, expedited shipping) and inability to meet all orders, thus reducing profitability and order fulfillment. Prompt #2: Ways to increase order fulfillment: reduce process/transportation/lead times, increase inventory levels, improve forecasting accuracy, enhance supplier engagement, optimize distribution/transportation planning. Trade-offs: increased inventory means higher working capital and inventory costs; reducing lead times might compromise process robustness or not be feasible; ramping up production could strain resources. Prompt #3: To prioritize customers and improve order fulfillment, the client should prioritize based on profitability per ton. Information required: revenues, costs (especially variable cost per ton), volumes ordered, and current volume fulfilled for each customer. Prompt #4: Using Exhibit 2 and a constant cost of 1,250 Euros/ton: Calculate Volume Fulfilled = Volume Ordered * Order Fulfilment %. Calculate Profitability/Ton = (Revenue/Volume Fulfilled) – 1250. Calculate Total Profitability = Profitability/Ton * Volume Fulfilled. Rank customers by Profitability/Ton. The candidate should observe that high-value customers (F, B, G) have lower fulfillment rates. To maximize profits, reallocate the total capacity (127 kt) to the most profitable customers first. Best case scenario: F (60 kt), B (45 kt), G (20 kt) get 100% fulfillment. The remaining 2 kt goes to C. This increases total profits from 212M to 326M Euros (54% increase) and ensures 100% fulfillment for the top 3. Prompt #5: Risks of focusing on top 3: loss of future potential/growth from de-prioritized customers, increased reliance on a few customers (volatility risk), reduced customer diversification, de-prioritized customers may switch suppliers. Mitigation: identify customer expectations (100% fulfillment may not be necessary), explore new production methods (outsourcing, new plant), increase lead time requirements in contracts, reduce orders, prioritize crucial parts, or create different supply chains for different products. Recommendation: Recommend a prioritization exercise and reallocation of volumes to the most profitable customers (F, B, G) to achieve 100% order fulfillment for them, increasing overall profits by 54%. Acknowledge risks of de-prioritizing other customers and propose mitigation strategies like careful communication, setting realistic expectations, and exploring alternative solutions for smaller customers. Implement a weekly process to monitor and coordinate.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate a structured approach to problem-solving, covering financial and operational aspects. They should be able to identify key trade-offs and accurately perform calculations from Exhibit 2 to determine profitability and reallocation. The final recommendation should be clear, actionable, and address both the quantitative and qualitative aspects of the case, including risks and mitigation strategies.

title: Critical Transportation
description: Your client, Critical Transportation (CT), provides logistical support for the rail industry on the West Coast of the United States. Due to the recent Panama Canal expansion, CT is expecting a shift in rail volume to the East Coast once the expanded canal opens in early 2016. CT has retained our firm’s services to determine if it should make the investment to expand its business to the East Coast.
additional_info: Objective: CT wants to determine if their current business model would be profitable on the East Coast; and whether entry into this market makes sense given the shifts in the market.
Current Market: Currently, CT is a market leader with 25% market share; there are 5 competitors in the West Coast market and the total market size is $2B.

Business Model: CT provides rail transportation services from major port locations on West Coast to distribution centers all over the Western U.S. Once cargo is loaded (completed by port personnel), they transport via rail to a final location where it is unloaded by their client. CT does not provide any additional services.

Trends: It is estimated that 25% of West Coast container traffic will shift to the East Coast beginning in 2016. Over time, the West and East Coast ports are expected to equalize in revenue earned per year.
Possible Market: There are only 3 other comparable companies on the East Coast that focus on CT’s core competency, and these companies have no West Coast presence.
Costs: CT estimates that entry into the East Coast market will cost a minimum of $450M, but once entered, East Coast operations will cost approximately the same as West Coast operations.
additional_questions: Our client is chiefly concerned about the profit margins in the East Coast market after its entry. Based on the provided information, what should CT’s profit margins be in the East Coast?

Our internal analysts believe that CT can only capture 10% of the East Coast market upon entry in the first year. The West Coast market size is $2B, and 25% of that market is expected to shift to the East Coast after the canal opens in 2016. The initial investment to begin operations on the East Coast would be approximately $450M. Based upon this information when would CT break even? Ignore time value of money and assume no growth.

Given that it would take many years to recoup initial costs unless CT could gain a larger market share, what are some other recommendations for CT to increase profitability in the East Coast after the Panama Canal expansion?

John Gray, CEO of Critical Transportation, is expected to walk in the room in about 5 minutes and wants to hear our thoughts about the direction his company should take in the future. What do you recommend?
solution: Prompt #1: The candidate should develop a market entry framework, considering external factors (market size, growth, competition, regulatory), revenue (pricing, volume, service offerings), costs (fixed, variable, benchmarking), and entry strategy (acquisition, JV, organic). Prompt #2a: From Exhibit 1, calculate the profit margin for each East Coast competitor: Company A: (40% of Total Market Revenue - $320M) / (40% of Total Market Revenue) = (0.4 * $1000M - $320M) / $400M = ($400M - $320M) / $400M = $80M / $400M = 20%. Similarly for B and C, the profit margin is 20%. So, CT's profit margin in the East Coast should be 20%. Prompt #2b: East Coast market in 2015 is $1B. 25% of West Coast traffic ($2B * 0.25 = $0.5B) shifts to East Coast. So, 2016 East Coast market size = $1B + $0.5B = $1.5B. CT's revenue in Y1 = 10% of $1.5B = $150M. CT's profit in Y1 = 20% of $150M = $30M. Break-even time = Initial Investment / Annual Profit = $450M / $30M = 15 years. Prompt #3: Recommendations to increase profitability: 1. Buy/Borrow: Acquire an existing East Coast player to gain market share quickly and leverage their infrastructure/customer base. 2. Expand service offerings: Provide value-added services beyond basic rail transport (e.g., repackaging, last-mile delivery, warehousing). 3. Diversify transportation modes: Explore trucking services to complement rail, offering more flexible solutions. 4. Aggressive customer acquisition: Leverage existing West Coast relationships and competitive pricing to capture more than 10% market share. 5. Strategic partnerships: Form alliances with East Coast logistics providers. Recommendation: CT should enter the East Coast market, but with a revised strategy to accelerate market share capture and profitability. The 15-year break-even period for organic entry is too long. Recommend pursuing an acquisition or joint venture with an existing East Coast player to immediately gain market share and reduce the break-even time. Additionally, CT should explore offering value-added services beyond basic rail transport to differentiate itself and capture higher margins. Risks include integration challenges with an acquisition, potential for cannibalization of existing services, and the need for significant capital investment. Next steps include identifying potential acquisition targets, conducting due diligence, and developing a detailed integration plan.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate a strong understanding of market entry and profitability analysis. They should accurately calculate profit margins and break-even periods. The recommendations should be creative, actionable, and directly address the client's concerns about profitability and market share. The final recommendation should be structured, concise, and provide a clear path forward with consideration of risks.

title: Dealer Jack’s
description: Your client is Dealer Jack’s, a large US based grocery retailer with stores located on the East Coast. Dealer Jack’s stores provide a full grocery offering with produce, deli, and fresh bakery departments and a mix of national and private label brands. The company, affectionately referred to as “DJ’s” by its customers, experienced strong growth for the better part of the past two decades outpacing the industry until recently. Over the past three years however, Dealer Jack’s annual profits have stagnated and even declined in the past year while the grocery industry has continued to grow with hard discounters such as Trader Joe’s leading the way. The CEO has been getting increasing pressure from the board to turn things around, and we have been hired to diagnose the cause of DJ’s profitability issues and find ways to improve it.
additional_info: Dealer Jack’s management has examined same store sales for all of its 300 locations, and profitability appears to be a pervasive issue across all stores. They do not believe targeting and closing underperforming stores is an appropriate solution.
While the Dealer Jack’s brand is strong on the East Coast, management sees geographic expansion as too capital intensive in the short term.
All non-operating expenses/costs are appropriately benchmarked to the industry.

Private label brands are commonly referred to as store brands like 365 at Whole Foods or Kirkland’s at Costco. Hard discounters such as Trader Joe’s rely heavily on private label brands. National brands come from CPG firms such as General Mills or Kraft. CPG firms require a margin on their products that’s not applicable to private label brands.
DJ’s had 2016 revenue of $60 billion with 30% gross margin and 3% profit margin.
Management wants to increase the profits by 10% within the next year. (Target profit increase is $180 million = $60 billion x 3% x 10%)
additional_questions: Only 33% of all Dealer Jack’s sales come from the higher margin private label brands compared to industry leader, Trader Joe’s, which has 45% penetration. Management expects revenues to be flat in the coming year, but believes that by re-focusing on private label brands, Dealer Jack’s could increase its private label sales penetration by 3 percentage points without losing any sales dollars.
